requirements_for_qualification.summary: Must establish SEP-IRA as self-employed individual or small business owner to contribute up to 25% of compensation or 20% of self-employment income providing significant tax-deferred retirement savings with minimal administrative burden
criteria: Self-employed individual, sole proprietor, partnership, or business owner can establish SEP-IRA
2025 contribution limit: Lesser of $70,000 or 25% of compensation (20% of self-employment income after SE tax deduction)
Employer contributions only; no employee deferrals permitted (unlike Solo 401k)
Must contribute same percentage for all eligible employees if have employees meeting eligibility requirements
Minimal paperwork: no annual filing requirements unlike 401(k) requiring Form 5500
Can establish and contribute up to tax filing deadline including extensions (October 15 for individuals)
Contributions immediately 100% vested; no vesting schedules permitted
Easy to establish with most financial institutions using IRS Form 5305-SEP

sep_ira_rules.high_contribution_limit: Up to $70,000 annually; 25% of compensation or 20% of self-employment income
sep_ira_rules.simple_administration: No annual Form 5500 filing; minimal compliance burden compared to 401(k)
sep_ira_rules.late_establishment: Can establish and contribute up to tax filing deadline with extensions
sep_ira_rules.immediate_vesting: All contributions 100% vested immediately; no forfeiture risk for employees
sep_ira_rules.flexible_contributions: Can vary contribution percentage year to year; no fixed commitment
sep_ira_rules.self_employment_calculation: 20% of net self-employment income after deducting 1/2 SE tax

restrictions: Employer contributions only; cannot make employee deferrals unlike Solo 401k or 401(k)
Must contribute same percentage for all eligible employees; cannot discriminate
Employee eligibility: must cover employees age 21+ with $750+ earnings in 3 of last 5 years
No loan provisions; cannot borrow from SEP-IRA unlike some 401(k) plans
No Roth option; all contributions pre-tax and distributions taxed as ordinary income
Cannot make age 50+ catch-up contributions; fixed percentage-of-compensation limit only
Lower contribution potential than Solo 401k for same self-employment income due to no employee deferral
RMDs required starting age 73 (75 if born 1960+); same as traditional IRA rules
